
(a.k.a. the Mantel-Cox test)

{{< slidebreak >}}

:::{#exr-compare-surv-ests}
How do we test the null hypothesis that two or more groups
have the same time-to-event distribution?
:::

{{< slidebreak >}}

:::{#sol-compare-surv-ests}
One option is the **log-rank test**,
which compares the Kaplan-Meier estimates of the survival functions of
those groups.
:::

{{< slidebreak >}}

::: notes
Adapted from [@kleinbaum2012survival, Section IV, pp. 67--68]:

- The log-rank test is a large-sample chi-square test.

- The log-rank test uses a test statistic that compares KM curves
between groups across all survival times.

- Like many other statistics used in chi-square tests,
the log-rank statistic compares observed versus expected cell counts
over categories of outcomes.

- The categories for the log-rank statistic
are defined by each of the ordered failure times for the entire set of
data being analyzed.

:::

## Notation {#sec-logrank-notation}

{{< include _subfiles/intro-to-survival-analysis/_sec-logrank-notation.qmd >}}

## Log-rank statistic (two groups)

{{< include _subfiles/intro-to-survival-analysis/_sec-logrank-stat-two-group.qmd >}}

## Log-rank statistic (more than two groups)

{{< include _subfiles/intro-to-survival-analysis/_sec-logrank-stat-multigroup.qmd >}}

## The `survdiff` function

```{r, printr.help.sections = c("description", "usage")}
?survdiff
```

{{< include _subfiles/intro-to-survival-analysis/_exm-survdiff-drug6mp.qmd >}}
